text.title: diary-study-basics Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for diary-study-basics. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Choose the study type based on what triggers a relevant event for your research question.
rule.rationale: Each type captures different behavioral signals. Mixing types within one study produces incommensurable data.

rule.statement: Cap study duration at 10-21 days for most products; 28+ days only for true habit-formation research.
rule.rationale: Participant fatigue causes 30-50% entry dropout after day 10 in studies without strong re-engagement. Studies longer than 3 weeks lose late entries to "same as yesterday" responses.

rule.statement: Each diary entry must take under 3 minutes to complete. If the form is longer, split into a quick log and a weekly deep-dive.
rule.rationale: Entry burden is the top predictor of dropout. Participants with demanding forms disengage before the study reaches the habit-formation window.

rule.statement: Define research questions before selecting study type or duration. The questions determine whether a diary study is the right method at all.
rule.rationale: Diary studies answer temporal and contextual questions. Using them for questions better answered by interviews or analytics wastes 2-4 weeks of participant effort.

rule.statement: Use the five-field entry structure for every study: When (date/time), Where (location/context), What (what happened), How (emotional state), Why (participant's interpretation).
rule.rationale: These five fields capture the complete context needed for temporal and contextual analysis. Omitting "Why" produces entries that describe events without meaning; omitting "Where" prevents contextual pattern analysis.

rule.statement: Screen participants for willingness to self-document consistently, not just availability. Participants who journal regularly complete studies at 2x the rate of those who do not.
rule.rationale: The biggest predictor of dropout is unfamiliarity with self-documentation. Selection bias from motivated self-reporters is real but acceptable compared to 50% dropout from unscreened participants.

rule.statement: Conduct a live onboarding session with each participant before the diary period starts. Test their understanding of the entry format with a practice entry.
rule.rationale: Participants who submit a practice entry during onboarding complete 40% more entries than those who only read instructions. The practice entry surfaces confusion before it becomes dropout.

rule.statement: Use rolling synthesis, not batch analysis. Run a theme-synthesis pass weekly from day 3 so emerging patterns can generate follow-up questions while participants are still active.
rule.rationale: Late synthesis produces a complete retrospective analysis of an unprobed dataset. Themes identified by day 7 become the raw material for exit interview questions on day 14.

rule.statement: Participant fatigue: dropoff after day 7-10 unless re-engaged. Studies >3 weeks lose 30-50% of entries.
rule.rationale: Study duration and entry format directly impact completion. Shorter, lighter studies with multimodal options yield richer datasets than longer, text-heavy ones.

rule.statement: Selection bias: people willing to journal twice daily for a month are not your average user.
rule.rationale: Screen for journaling comfort and consistency as a research variable, not a bug. Acknowledge selection bias in findings interpretation.

rule.statement: Self-report drift: entries early in the study are detailed; late entries become "same as yesterday".
rule.rationale: Quality metrics like entry length and detail are signals of engagement and thoroughness. Weight late entries lower or re-engage participants at mid-study.

rule.statement: Recall bias on event-contingent prompts: "log every time you used the app" → users batch-log at end of day, losing in-the-moment context.
rule.rationale: Immediate prompts (push notifications, random signal-contingent) reduce recall drift. Trade-off: more interruption for better fidelity. Choose based on research question priorities.

rule.statement: Cap the study at 10-21 days for most products; 28+ days only for true habit-formation research.
rule.rationale: Longer studies add data overhead without proportional insight gain. Study duration should match research question temporal scope.

rule.statement: Send mid-study check-in calls (15 min) to catch confusion and rebuild commitment. This single intervention lifts compliance noticeably.
rule.rationale: Human contact mid-study signals that effort matters and gives participants a chance to clarify confusion before they disengage.

rule.statement: Pay in tranches: e.g. 30% on completion of week 1, 70% at study end. Pre-paid full incentive correlates with mid-study dropout.
rule.rationale: Incentive structure shapes completion. Staged incentives maintain commitment through the middle slump.

rule.statement: Mix entry formats: text + photo + 30-sec voice. Different participants engage with different modalities; mixed corpora are richer for synthesis.
rule.rationale: Multimodal intake captures context that single-modality studies miss (visual environments, vocal tone, emotional state).

rule.statement: Always conclude with an exit interview (30-45 min) per participant; the diary entries become the interview script.
rule.rationale: Exit interviews validate diary findings, surface misinterpretations, and provide narrative context for quantitative patterns.
